# Preface {.unnumbered}

Matt — thank you for the careful read of the proposal and for the five comments you left in the
margin. They land on exactly the right places. This short book is my answer. Its only goal is to
make **Pipeline B** and especially **Pipeline C** *legible*: to show, from four independent angles,
that they do what the proposal claims, and in particular to answer your central question —
**where does the external total variance $T_{\mathrm{MI}}$ come from, and does it secretly require
fitting the model many times?**

The one-sentence answer, which the rest of the book unpacks, is:

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**The short version.** $T_{\mathrm{MI}}$ *is* model-specific and it *does* require many fits — but
those are **cheap, fast `lme4` REML replicate refits** ($M(R{+}1)$ of them), **not** MCMC runs.
That cost is **shared by all three pipelines**; Pipeline C's saving lives entirely in the
**number of MCMC runs (ten → one)**. Nothing about the variance target is unique to Pipeline C,
and nothing about it is free — it is the same BRR-Fay machinery the field already runs.
:::

## How this book is organized

Each chapter is short and aimed at one question. The book answers from four angles in turn:

1. **Mathematics** — the identity that licenses the point estimate, and the exact origin of the
   variance target (Part II).
2. **Code** — the actual functions that implement each operator, mapped one-to-one to the math
   (Part III).
3. **A controlled simulation** — synthetic data with a *known* ground truth, where we watch
   Pipeline C recover the truth and reproduce Pipeline A (Part IV).
4. **PISA 2022** — the three pipelines run side by side on the real data, USA and Korea (Part V).

## Your five comments, and where each is answered

This table is the map of the whole book; @sec-response closes it back out at the end.

| Your comment (verbatim) | Where it points | Short answer | Chapter |
|---|---|---|---|
| *"How do we get this without using A or B methods?"* (**C2**, on $T_{\mathrm{MI}}$) | Pipeline C variance | The target is assembled by a **separate, cheap** BRR-Fay computation that every pipeline shares — not by the MCMC. | @sec-tmi |
| *"I don't understand where this comes from? isn't it model specific and needs … estimating multiple models?"* (**C4**) | Pipeline C variance | Yes — model-specific, and it is $M(R{+}1)$ **REML/GLS replicate refits** (seconds each), not ten MCMC runs (hours each). | @sec-tmi |
| *"Isn't this for the plausible values $y$ not the model parameters (betas)? How do we connect … to the variance of the parameters?"* (**C3**) | PV vs parameter variance | Each plausible value $y^{(m)}$ is pushed through the regression to a $\hat\beta_m$; the spread of those $\hat\beta_m$ **is** the parameter-space image of measurement uncertainty ($B$). | @sec-pv2par |
| *"why not do importance resampling for the joint distribution instead of the conditional/profile version?"* (**C5**) | conditional vs joint | The identity is exact only for the **betas** (their GLS gradient is linear in $y$); variance-component scores are not. Joint resampling is exactly what **Pipeline B's PSIS** does — we show both. | @sec-ccc |
| *"For importance sampling we may want to … temper to get better proposal values … include the modes from the replicate distributions. … double check the pareto version."* (**C1**) | Pipeline B proposal | We adopt your idea: we show how $1/M$, $1/2M$, $1/3M$ tempering flattens the proposal and what it does to the Pareto $\hat k$ diagnostic. | @sec-pipeb, @sec-tempering |

## A note on scope

This is a **demonstration**, not the manuscript. The simulations are deliberately at a
**smoke / pilot** scale — small enough to run quickly and transparently, large enough to make the
point. The aim is clarity and correctness, not the final production numbers. Where a claim is
exact, we prove it; where it is approximate, we say so and show the diagnostic that flags it.

*— JoonHo*
